explicit_text: I grew up in Japan until I was 14 years old, raised by my mother after my father left for America. I remember my childhood there, playing with my friends and going to grammar school. I went back to Japan three times after coming to America, and my old friends would come to see me. Japan was where my roots were, but I eventually settled in America to build a future.
implicit_text: I grew up in my home country until I was 14 years old, raised by my mother after my father left for America. I remember my childhood there, playing with my friends and going to grammar school. I went back there three times after coming to America, and my old friends would come to see me. That country was where my roots were, but I eventually settled in America to build a future.

explicit_text: My father was a foreman of a fruit ranch in Napa, California, and that’s where I first came when I arrived in America at age 14. I worked there as a farm laborer and later became a foreman myself. Napa was the place where I started my American life and learned the hard work of farming. It was also where I returned after working in other places like Oregon and Arizona.
implicit_text: My father was a foreman of a fruit ranch in a famous wine-producing region in California, and that’s where I first came when I arrived in America at age 14. I worked there as a farm laborer and later became a foreman myself. That region was the place where I started my American life and learned the hard work of farming. It was also where I returned after working in other places like Oregon and Arizona.

explicit_text: During the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, many people from Chiba Prefecture fled the city and came to my father’s ranch in Napa. My father helped them by giving food and work, such as picking pears and working in the dry yard. I heard about this from my father, who was already in America at that time. It was a difficult time, but my father showed kindness to those displaced by the disaster.
implicit_text: During the major 1906 earthquake in a northern California city, many people from my home prefecture fled the city and came to my father’s ranch in that famous California valley. My father helped them by giving food and work, such as picking pears and working in the dry yard. I heard about this from my father, who was already in America at that time. It was a difficult time, but my father showed kindness to those displaced by the disaster.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Chiba Prefecture, where I lived with my mother and siblings until I was 14. My passport was issued from the Chiba Prefectural office when I came to America. Many people from Chiba fled San Francisco after the 1906 earthquake and came to my father’s ranch in Napa. Chiba is where my family roots are, and it shaped my early years before moving to the U.S.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a coastal prefecture near Tokyo, where I lived with my mother and siblings until I was 14. My passport was issued from the prefectural office there when I came to America. Many people from that prefecture fled the 1906 earthquake in San Francisco and came to my father’s ranch in Napa. That prefecture is where my family roots are, and it shaped my early years before moving to the U.S.

explicit_text: San Francisco was the city affected by the 1906 earthquake, which caused many people from Chiba Prefecture to flee to Napa where my father worked. Although I never lived in San Francisco, I heard stories about the earthquake and its impact on the Japanese community there. San Francisco was a place of hardship for many immigrants at that time.
implicit_text: The northern California city on the bay was the place affected by the 1906 earthquake, which caused many people from my home prefecture to flee to Napa where my father worked. Although I never lived in that city, I heard stories about the earthquake and its impact on the Japanese community there. That city was a place of hardship for many immigrants at that time.

explicit_text: My sister’s youngest daughter, Yasuko, married a man from Hiroshima and has been living in Sacramento for several years. She visits us every week without fail. Although I am not from Hiroshima myself, the family connection through marriage keeps us linked to that place.
implicit_text: My sister’s youngest daughter, Yasuko, married a man from a southwestern Japanese city known for its peace memorial and has been living in California’s capital for several years. She visits us every week without fail. Although I am not from that city myself, the family connection through marriage keeps us linked to that place.

explicit_text: My brother moved to Sacramento to grow strawberries after we paid off our ranch in Oregon. Yasuko, my niece from my sister’s family, has been living in Sacramento for a few years and visits us regularly. Sacramento was one of the places where my family worked and settled during our farming years.
implicit_text: My brother moved to the capital city of California to grow strawberries after we paid off our ranch in Oregon. Yasuko, my niece from my sister’s family, has been living in that capital city for a few years and visits us regularly. That city was one of the places where my family worked and settled during our farming years.

explicit_text: My brother worked in Los Angeles for a while, but he caught measles there, so I went to convince him to move to North Pomona to pick oranges with me for his health. Los Angeles was a place where my brother sought work, but it was not ideal for him due to illness. I cared for him and helped him find better work nearby.
implicit_text: My brother worked in a major Southern California city for a while, but he caught measles there, so I went to convince him to move to a nearby town known for orange groves to pick fruit with me for his health. That city was a place where my brother sought work, but it was not ideal for him due to illness. I cared for him and helped him find better work nearby.

explicit_text: In the Imperial Valley, my brother and I worked on a 40-acre cantaloupe ranch under contract. The weather was extremely hot, reaching 115 degrees, and the wind felt like fire. We worked very hard, often until 10 at night, picking and packing fruit. It was tough work but an important part of our farming experience.
implicit_text: In a desert valley in Southern California, my brother and I worked on a 40-acre cantaloupe ranch under contract. The weather was extremely hot, reaching 115 degrees, and the wind felt like fire. We worked very hard, often until 10 at night, picking and packing fruit. It was tough work but an important part of our farming experience.

explicit_text: After working in Imperial Valley, my brother and I moved to Arizona, specifically Phoenix, to work on a ranch owned by Mr. Yamamoto, a Waseda University graduate. We worked there for about 20 days doing piecework, and I experienced a big thunderstorm that blew away our camp. Arizona was another challenging but memorable place in my farming journey.
implicit_text: After working in that desert valley, my brother and I moved to a southwestern state, specifically its capital city, to work on a ranch owned by Mr. Yamamoto, a graduate of a prestigious Tokyo university. We worked there for about 20 days doing piecework, and I experienced a big thunderstorm that blew away our camp. That state was another challenging but memorable place in my farming journey.

explicit_text: In Phoenix, Arizona, I worked on Mr. Yamamoto’s ranch, who was a graduate of Waseda University. We used horses to farm 200 acres, and I remember a fierce thunderstorm that destroyed our camp one night. Phoenix was a hot and tough place to work, but I was young and determined to make a living.

explicit_text: Many people smuggled themselves into America from Mexico during the time my brother and I worked in Imperial Valley. We were among the few who had passports, but others did not. I remember visiting a young man named Nakamura who was caught and jailed in Brawley before being sent back to Mexico. It was a difficult situation for many immigrants seeking work.
implicit_text: Many people smuggled themselves into America from the country south of the U.S. during the time my brother and I worked in that desert valley. It was a difficult situation for many immigrants seeking work.

explicit_text: Mr. Yamamoto, the ranch owner in Phoenix where I worked, was a graduate of Waseda University. He was a frank and hearty man who managed a large 200-acre ranch using horses. Knowing that he was educated at Waseda gave me respect for him and his leadership on the ranch.

explicit_text: My brother and I worked picking oranges in North Pomona before moving on to Imperial Valley. After my brother got sick in Los Angeles, I convinced him to join me in North Pomona for better health. We worked at Mr. Sasaki’s camp there until June, and it was a quieter, healthier place to work.

explicit_text: Immigrants had to get their passports from the Kanagawa Prefectural office, but I received mine from Chiba Prefectural office as a non-immigrant. This distinction was important for immigration paperwork when I came to America. Kanagawa Prefecture was the usual place for immigrant passports, but my case was different.

explicit_text: I attended Heald College for a while when I was about 25 years old, after working in farming for some years. I quit the college when I went back to Japan to meet a prospective bride. Although I didn’t finish, going to Heald College was part of my efforts to improve myself beyond farming.

explicit_text: When I was 27 years old, the Woodbridge Act passed Congress in 1924, which prohibited Japanese from entering America. This law affected many Japanese immigrants like myself. It was a difficult time politically, and it influenced my decisions about marriage and returning to Japan.
implicit_text: When I was 27 years old, a U.S. law passed in 1924 that prohibited people from my home country from entering America. This law affected many immigrants like myself. It was a difficult time politically, and it influenced my decisions about marriage and returning to my home country.
